exact claim: Everything about the DTC hangs on four words: severe and prolonged. They don't mean what most people assume, and that misunderstanding is why people who qualify never apply.
“Severe” is not about your diagnosis

The CRA never asks how serious your condition sounds. It asks how much it limits you day to day. Two people with the identical diagnosis can get opposite answers — and someone with a condition you've never heard of can qualify easily. Never rule yourself out because your diagnosis doesn't sound bad enough.
You're judged with your treatment, not without it

The test is whether you're still limited even with appropriate therapy, medication and devices. So the honest answer to “but I manage okay with my meds” is: that's exactly the question. If you're still restricted while doing everything right, that counts.
You don't have to be unable — just slow

“Markedly restricted” means unable to do something or taking an inordinate amount of time to do it. CRA's own yardstick is roughly three times longer than someone without the impairment. Taking forever to dress, walk, or organise your day is the thing being measured, not failure to do it at all.
“All or substantially all of the time” = about 90%

This is where fluctuating conditions get lost. If you have good days, the question is not “are you always like this?” — it's whether the restriction is there roughly 90% of the time. Describe your typical day, not your best one, and say plainly how often the bad days come.
Two smaller limits can add up to one big one

This is the route most people miss. If you're significantly limited in two or more categories and the combined effect is as severe as one marked restriction, you can qualify on cumulative effect. CRA's own example: always slow to walk and always slow to dress. Ask your practitioner about it by name.
“Prolonged” just means 12 months
It has lasted, or is expected to last, a continuous 12 months. It does not mean permanent, and it does not mean forever — an impairment expected to last a year counts.

The categories the CRA scores are: walking, dressing, feeding, speaking, hearing, seeing, eliminating (bladder or bowel), mental functions, and life-sustaining therapy. Only your practitioner can say where you land — your job is to make sure they're describing your limitations, not your label.
a medical practitioner confirming your condition markedly restricts a basic daily activity (or would, without therapy) all or substantially all of the time, for 12+ months
correction: Limit the without-therapy alternative to the official life-sustaining-therapy test; do not imply ordinary treatment response satisfies it.
